sections.text: Welcome back to AP Psychology. Module Ten — developmental psychology. This is the unit about how a single cell becomes a thinking, feeling, identity-having human, and what changes — and what doesn't — across an entire lifespan. By the end of today you'll know the four big developmental theorists AP loves to test, and you'll stop mixing up Piaget's four stages with Erikson's eight. Let's get into it.

Quick reality check. According to Erik Erikson, you — sitting there watching this — are smack in the middle of a stage called identity versus role confusion. Right now. The biggest psychological task of your life, by Erikson's framework, is figuring out who you actually are, separate from your parents, your friends, your group chats. So this whole module isn't just abstract theory about babies and old people. The middle of it is partly about you. The struggles you're having with 'who am I' — that's a documented developmental stage. Let's see how all the pieces fit together.

Three things today. One — the three domains of development and how a human gets built from a zygote to a senior citizen. Two — Piaget's four stages of cognitive development, plus Vygotsky's alternative. Three — Erikson, Kohlberg, attachment, and how the mind ages. Big unit. We're moving fast. Buckle in.

Development happens in three domains at once. Physical — your body, brain, motor skills. Cognitive — thinking, language, memory, reasoning. Social-emotional — relationships, identity, feelings. They don't develop in isolation. A toddler's exploding vocabulary is cognitive, but it's also social — they're learning to connect — and physical, because their brain is literally wiring itself. Now, before birth — three prenatal stages. Zygote in the first two weeks, embryo from week two to eight, fetus from week nine to birth. The big AP word here — teratogens — agents that cross the placenta and damage development. The textbook example is alcohol, which causes fetal alcohol syndrome. Memorize that link — alcohol equals F-A-S.

Now infancy. Babies arrive with built-in reflexes — rooting, the grasp reflex, the Moro or startle reflex. But the most famous infancy research isn't about reflexes — it's about attachment. Harry Harlow's nineteen fifties monkeys. Baby monkeys were given two fake mothers — a wire one with a bottle of food, and a soft cloth one with no food. The babies clung to the cloth mother and only ran to the wire one to feed. The takeaway shocked the field — contact comfort matters more than food. Then Mary Ainsworth's Strange Situation experiment found four attachment styles. Secure — baby's upset when mom leaves, calmed when she returns. Most kids, healthiest pattern. Insecure-avoidant — baby ignores mom either way. Insecure-anxious, sometimes called resistant — baby is upset when mom leaves and stays upset, even pushing her away when she returns. And disorganized — no clear strategy, often linked to abuse or trauma. Four styles. AP loves them. Lock them in.

Piaget. Jean Piaget. Cognitive development happens in four stages — and AP wants you to know all four with their key milestones. Stage one — Sensorimotor, birth to two. The big milestone — object permanence — understanding that things still exist when out of sight. Before this, peekaboo blows their mind because the face literally vanished. Stage two — Preoperational, two to seven. Symbolic thinking explodes. They use words and pictures, but two big limits. Egocentrism — they can't take your perspective. A four-year-old hides their face and thinks you can't see them. And lack of conservation — pour the same juice from a short fat cup into a tall thin one, they'll swear the tall one has more. Stage three — Concrete operational, seven to eleven. They get conservation. They get reversibility. They can do logic — but only on real, concrete things. Stage four — Formal operational, eleven and up. Abstract reasoning, hypothetical thinking, what-ifs. Quick mnemonic — Some Pretty Cool Frogs. Sensorimotor, Preoperational, Concrete, Formal. In age order. Use it on the exam.

Piaget had a rival. Russian psychologist Lev Vygotsky said cognitive development isn't a kid-alone-with-their-brain process — it's social and cultural. Kids learn from people and tools handed to them by their culture. His big idea — the Zone of Proximal Development, Z-P-D. Picture three rings. Inner ring — what a child can already do alone. Outer ring — what's still impossible even with help. The middle ring is the magic — what they can do WITH help from someone more skilled. That middle ring is the zone of proximal development. The technique that lifts them through it is called scaffolding — give just enough support, then pull it back as they get stronger. Like training wheels. The AP contrast — Piaget says development drives learning. Vygotsky says learning, especially social and cultural learning, drives development.

Your turn. A six-year-old watches you pour juice from a wide short cup into a tall narrow cup. No juice is added. No juice is removed. They watch the whole thing. You ask — does the tall cup have more, less, or the same amount of juice? They say — the tall one has more. Question one — what cognitive limit is this? Question two — which of Piaget's stages tells you this child is in? Pause. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. The limit is called lack of conservation — the child doesn't yet understand that quantity stays the same when shape changes. The stage is preoperational, ages two to seven. A six-year-old fits. By concrete operational — age seven plus — they would conserve, and they'd say 'same amount, you just moved it.' Two AP details to lock in. One — egocentrism and lack of conservation are both preoperational limits. Two — the moment they conserve, they've crossed into concrete operational. The conservation task is the single most-tested experiment in this whole unit.

Erikson. Eight stages of psychosocial development — meaning each stage is a social and emotional crisis you have to resolve. AP doesn't usually drill you on all eight, but you need the pattern and the highlights. Stage one — infancy — trust versus mistrust. Can I count on the world? Stage two — toddler — autonomy versus shame and doubt. Can I do things myself? Stage three — preschool — initiative versus guilt. Stage four — elementary school — industry versus inferiority. Stage five — adolescence — identity versus role confusion. This one. The AP favorite. Who am I? What's my role? Stage six — young adulthood — intimacy versus isolation. Can I form deep connections? Stage seven — middle adulthood — generativity versus stagnation. Am I contributing? Stage eight — late adulthood — integrity versus despair. Looking back, was my life meaningful? Eight crises. One per life chapter. Quick mnemonic — pair each with the rough age — and the one circled in red on every AP exam is stage five, identity versus role confusion, adolescence.

Kohlberg. Lawrence Kohlberg studied moral reasoning by giving people moral dilemmas — most famously, the Heinz dilemma — should a man steal a drug to save his dying wife? It's not the answer that mattered to Kohlberg — it's the reasoning behind it. He found three levels. Preconventional — typical of young children — morality is about avoiding punishment and getting rewards. 'Don't steal because you'll get caught.' Conventional — most teens and adults — morality is about social rules and what others think. 'Don't steal because it's against the law' or 'because good people don't steal.' Postconventional — some adults, not all — morality is about abstract principles, even when they conflict with the law. 'Steal the drug, because human life outweighs property rights.' Now the critique you need to know. Carol Gilligan argued Kohlberg's research was male-biased. She said women tend to reason morally through care and relationships, not abstract justice — and Kohlberg's scoring system penalized that. AP loves pairing Kohlberg's three levels with Gilligan's critique.

Last big idea — aging and the mind. Two kinds of intelligence. Crystallized intelligence — your accumulated knowledge, vocabulary, expertise — actually stays stable or grows into old age. Grandparents crushing you at trivia? That's crystallized. Fluid intelligence — your raw processing speed, working memory, ability to solve new problems — peaks in your twenties and slowly declines after that. Memorize the pair. Crystallized — stays or grows. Fluid — declines. Older adults compensate by leaning on crystallized. They know more, even if they process slower. The cognitive-decline cliché — old people can't learn — is mostly wrong. Different intelligences age differently.

Recap. Six things. One — three domains of development: physical, cognitive, social-emotional, all happening at once. Prenatal stages: zygote, embryo, fetus. Teratogens like alcohol cause F-A-S. Two — attachment: Harlow showed contact comfort beats food. Ainsworth's four styles: secure, avoidant, anxious, disorganized. Three — Piaget's four cognitive stages: sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, formal operational. Object permanence, egocentrism, conservation, abstract thought. Four — Vygotsky: zone of proximal development plus scaffolding. Learning is social and cultural. Five — Erikson's eight psychosocial stages. The AP favorite is identity versus role confusion in adolescence. Kohlberg's three levels of moral reasoning, plus Gilligan's male-bias critique. Six — aging: crystallized intelligence stays or grows, fluid intelligence declines.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Ten, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, Module Ten on developmental psychology. Pay extra attention to the Piaget stages and the Erikson stages — that's where AP traps you. Two — go to AP Classroom and try fifteen multiple-choice questions matching scenarios to either a Piaget stage or an Erikson stage. Practice telling them apart. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: read ten short scenarios — a child failing a conservation task, a teenager questioning their career, an older adult reviewing their life — and tag each with the correct theorist and stage. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if Piaget versus Erikson still feels fuzzy. They're the most-confused pair in the whole AP curriculum. See you in Module Eleven — personality, where we figure out why you and your sibling, raised in the same house, came out wildly different humans.
